So happiness is not a result, but an ability: the ability to notice the small good things in life.
Rather than envying other people's lives, it is better to earnestly live your own today well.
What is happiness? Perhaps it is hiding in the cup of hot tea you drink today.
Over six hundred years ago, in the Ming dynasty, China had a great navigator named Zheng He.
From 1405 to 1433, he led fleets on seven voyages to the Western Seas, visiting more than thirty countries and regions.
His fleet was enormous in scale: at its largest it had over two hundred ships and more than twenty-seven thousand people.
The largest of these ships were called "treasure ships"; they are said to have been over a hundred meters long, like palaces on the sea.
The fleet carried silk, porcelain, and tea abroad, and also brought back spices and other local specialties from many lands.
Most interestingly, on one voyage the fleet brought back a giraffe, and the whole city ran to see this magical animal.
Nearly everywhere the fleet went, it left behind friendly memories.
After Zheng He passed away, ocean voyages on such a large scale came to an end.
Today people still commemorate him, because he proved that the ocean can link the world together.
Translation is far from a simple matter of swapping the words of one language for those of another; it is the art of building a bridge between two cultures.
In the West there is a famous saying — "to translate is to betray" — because no translation can ever be completely faithful to the original.
Take the word yuanfen: English has no ready-made equivalent, so the translator can only spend a whole sentence explaining this connection too wonderful for words.
Likewise, translate English humor literally into Chinese and the joke usually vanishes without a trace; a masterly translator will start from scratch and make readers laugh in Chinese's own way.
The translation world has a three-character standard — xin, da, ya: be faithful to the original meaning, read smoothly and fluently, and preserve the spirit of the original.
The three often conflict with one another, and the translator is forced to make painful trade-offs between faithfulness and beauty.
The reason we call translation an art rather than a technique is that it has no single correct answer: every translation is an act of re-creation.
The moment you try translating your favorite line of poetry from your mother tongue into Chinese, you will immediately taste the translator's joys and hardships.
Western languages pursue precision, yet Chinese often excels at vagueness; this vagueness is by no means a defect but a beauty all its own.
A friend asks what time you will arrive, and you answer "around eight"; the host says "sit wherever you like," and the guest says "I'll just have a little of whatever."
A single "more or less" both gives the other person a rough expectation and leaves yourself some leeway — two birds with one stone, as it were.
Chinese painting values empty space, and the Chinese language likewise values what lies beyond the words: say three-tenths, and leave the other seven-tenths for the listener to work out.
Or take "I'll treat you to dinner some other day": the "some other day" is most likely not a concrete date but a way of expressing goodwill.
If beginners take all these phrases at face value, misunderstandings are inevitable; only by grasping the tacit understanding behind the vagueness have you truly crossed the threshold.
Of course, vagueness has its limits: when signing contracts or negotiating prices, Chinese people will haggle over every ounce, with no vagueness whatsoever.
Does your mother tongue also have expressions that "say one half and hold back the other"? Compare them, and you will see both cultures more clearly.
With high-rises standing like forests and streets streaming with traffic, big cities attract countless dream-chasing young people with their bustle and prosperity.
Behind the prosperity, however, 'big-city disease' is becoming increasingly prominent.
Traffic congestion devours people's time, and soaring housing prices leave young people gasping for breath.
Air pollution and noise disturbance further leave urban residents' quality of life greatly compromised.
Some choose to flee 'Beijing, Shanghai, and Guangzhou' and return to smaller cities with a gentler pace.
Others believe that the opportunities and resources of big cities remain irreplaceable.
In fact, the root of the problem lies not in city size itself, but in the imbalance of resource distribution.
Only by promoting coordinated regional development and making smaller cities equally good places to live and work can people truly have the freedom to choose.
Should a city's value, after all, be measured by its height, or by its residents' sense of happiness?
We like to assume our judgments are objective and rational, yet psychological research shows the human brain comes equipped with all sorts of "cognitive biases."
A cognitive bias is a systematic deviation that occurs when the mind processes information.
Here is a common example: after buying a car of a certain brand, you suddenly notice the same model everywhere on the street.
It is not that there are suddenly more of those cars; rather, your attention is being pulled by "confirmation bias," so you only see evidence that supports your own choice.
Another example: people tend to overestimate their own ability, and paradoxically, the more ignorant a person is, the more confident they tend to be — the so-called "Dunning-Kruger effect."
The reason cognitive biases are so hard to overcome is that they operate outside our awareness; we cannot perceive that we are being swayed by them.

The good news is that although biases can never be fully eliminated, they can be weakened through practice: ask yourself one more question — "Could I be wrong?" — and actively look for evidence against your view.
Admitting that you can be wrong is nothing to be ashamed of; what is truly dangerous is knowing nothing about your own blind spots.
In traditional Chinese culture, collective interests are often placed above individual interests.
As the saying goes, 'the flame leaps high when everyone gathers firewood' — unity and cooperation can indeed create achievements no individual could ever reach.
However, overemphasizing the collective inevitably suppresses individuality, preventing personal creativity from being fully brought into play.
Conversely, Western societies uphold individualism, yet as a result face the predicament of fading human warmth and declining social cohesion.
From this we can see that the collective and the individual are by no means incompatible like fire and water, but rather complement each other.
A healthy society must both safeguard individual freedom and dignity, and cultivate citizens' sense of responsibility.
The key lies in striking the right balance: the collective should not engulf the individual, nor should the individual place itself above the collective.
So when collective and individual interests clash, how exactly should we choose between them?
Bankers understand the power of compound interest: let a small sum keep rolling over, and in a few decades it becomes a considerable fortune.
Habits, in fact, obey the very same law.
Twenty minutes of reading a day adds up to over a hundred hours a year — enough to chew through a dozen thick books.
Conversely, an hour of late-night scrolling every day, accumulated over months and years, overdraws your entire store of energy and health.
The frightening thing about a habit is that its effects are nearly invisible in the short run; good or bad, they only reveal themselves years later.

The British cycling team was once an obscure squad; its new coach pursued no grand reforms, demanding only a one-percent improvement in every detail — and within a few years the team was, astonishingly, sweeping Olympic medals.
Clearly, what determines the course of a life is seldom the grand, dramatic decisions, but the unremarkable little choices made every day.
A habit is a compound-interest account that time opens in our name: whatever you deposit today, you will harvest tomorrow.
In the Tao Te Ching, Laozi wrote: "He who knows contentment is rich."
Read today, more than two thousand years later, these words remain deeply thought-provoking.
Our material conditions far surpass those of the past, yet our sense of happiness has not necessarily grown with them.
Psychologists call this "hedonic adaptation": the thrill of a new car evaporates within three months, and people promptly fix their eyes on the next target.
I know an old carpenter in the countryside who lives in an old house and works with old tools, yet is always beaming; he says: "Enough is enough — craving more only wears you out."
Contentment is not a lack of ambition; it is knowing exactly what is worth chasing and what is merely the burden of vanity.
Desire is like seawater — the more you drink, the thirstier you become; gratitude is like a clear spring — a gentle stream that never runs dry.
Happiness was never on some faraway wish list; it lies in the everyday things we see the moment we lower our heads.
In today's fast-paced society, the term "craftsman spirit" seems somewhat out of step with the times.
And yet it is precisely those craftsmen — patient with solitude, forever striving for perfection — who have created countless breathtaking works.
In Japan there is a sushi master named Jiro Ono; after more than seventy years of making sushi, he is still pondering how to get the temperature of the rice exactly right.
When someone asked him the secret of his success, he said only this: "Do simple things over and over, and do the things you repeat with your whole heart."
The craftsman spirit is by no means stubborn conservatism; it is a pursuit of quality that borders on the ruthless.
At the same time, it is an attitude toward life: refusing to be swayed by restless trends, and focusing on each step of the work at hand.
In an age when everyone chases instant results, slowing down has, paradoxically, become a rare ability.
Craft skills may age, but the craftsman's heart never dies.
In an age of information explosion, attention has become one of our scarcest resources.
Many people bustle about all day, only to find that their genuinely important work has barely moved forward.
The reason is simple: their time has been sliced into fragments by email, meetings, and endless notifications.
So-called "deep work" is the ability to concentrate without distraction on a single high-value task for an extended period.
A writer friend of mine has an instructive habit: every morning he locks his phone in a drawer, writes for three hours straight, and only then deals with trivial matters.
As a result, what he finished in one year exceeded the previous three years combined.
Deep work is rare precisely because it goes against the brain's instinct to seek fresh stimulation, and therefore requires deliberate training.
Once you have tasted the rewards of total absorption, it becomes hard to tolerate shallow, fragmented busyness again.
Rather than complaining that there is never enough time, ask yourself: did you set aside one whole, uninterrupted block of time today?
For the past several decades, globalization was regarded as an irreversible tide of history.
Goods, capital, and talent flowed freely across borders, as if the world had become a 'global village.'
In recent years, however, trade protectionism has reared its head, and quite a few countries have built tariff barriers one after another.
The shock of the pandemic further made countries realize that over-reliance on global supply chains harbors enormous risks.
Some people therefore assert that globalization has reached its end.
In my view, this claim is rather an exaggeration.
What is receding is only the old model; in the face of shared challenges such as climate change, the necessity of cooperation is only increasing.
Globalization will not die out; rather, it is seeking a new balance amid twists and turns.
Facing the receding tide, do we choose to build walls, or to build bridges?
In the 1960s, psychologists at Stanford University conducted the famous "marshmallow experiment."
The researchers gave each child a marshmallow and told them: if you can hold out for fifteen minutes without eating it, you will get a second one.
Some children stuffed the candy into their mouths at once; others shut their eyes or sang to distract themselves, and by sheer willpower endured the long wait.
Decades of follow-up showed that the children who could delay gratification generally went on to do better in both school and career.
Delaying gratification is not repressing desire; it is knowing clearly that some rewards are worth trading patience for.
Regrettably, modern life tempts us at every turn to "enjoy it now": takeout arrives in half an hour, short videos switch every three seconds, and even knowledge is sold as "mastered in ten minutes."
The more easily a pleasure is gained, the more quickly it vanishes; the longer a fruit must be waited for, the sweeter it tastes.
Life is like brewing wine: no fierce flame can force out the mellow aroma — only time can bring the flavor to perfection.
The moment 'the Mean' is mentioned, many people think of mediocrity, slickness, even spineless fence-sitting.
This is truly a misunderstanding that has lasted two thousand years.
The Mean that Confucius spoke of centers on 'excess is as bad as deficiency': overdoing a thing and underdoing it are equally harmful.
The Mean is by no means the absence of a stance; it is finding, between two extremes, the fulcrum that is exactly right.
It is like simmering a pot of soup: too fierce a flame boils it dry, too low a flame leaves it raw — what a good chef cares about is the control of heat.
In modern life this sense of proportion is everywhere: work hard, but not to the point of overdrawing your health; be sincere with people, but you need not be brutally blunt in every sentence.
In the internet age, black-or-white debates prevail and people rush to pick a side; the middle ground, paradoxically, has become the loneliest place to stand.
Yet truth seldom lives in the loudspeakers at either end; it lives in the quiet middle.
The Mean is not lacking the courage to go to extremes; it is having the power to go to extremes and choosing restraint — and that is what makes it so refined.
Every language has euphemisms, but the Chinese tradition of speaking indirectly runs especially deep: some things cannot be said straight out and must take a detour.

The most typical cases involve age and death: saying someone is "getting on in years" is far more tactful than calling him "old," while "passed away" and "has gone" stand in for that unbearably heavy word "die."

In daily life, someone heading to the restroom will say "I'll just go take care of something convenient," and to talk someone out of a plan, people often say "I'm afraid that might not be quite appropriate."
Workplace indirectness is an art in itself: when the boss says "think it over a bit more," it most likely means he does not approve of your proposal.
The reason euphemism is so highly developed is that Chinese culture places enormous weight on face: a point-blank refusal leaves both sides with no way to climb down gracefully.
So when Chinese people refuse someone, they rarely say "no"; instead they say "I'll do my best" or "let's see when the time comes," and the listener naturally reads between the lines.
For learners, understanding euphemisms is admittedly important, but more important still is learning to use them, so as not to offend anyone without meaning to.
Think about your own language: when you would rather not say "die" or "no" outright, what roundabout expressions do you reach for?
Modern life seems caught in a paradox: transport gets ever faster and communication ever more convenient, yet we always feel short of time.
Speed has admittedly brought efficiency, but it has also cost many people the room to think calmly.
Italy once saw the rise of a "slow food" movement, urging people to sit down and truly savor a meal instead of hastily silencing their stomachs with fast food.
The point of the movement was not to oppose speed itself, but to remind us that the quality of life cannot be measured by speed alone.
Fast and slow are in fact dialectical: drag your feet when speed is needed and you miss opportunities; rush when patience is needed and you plant hidden dangers.
Take learning a skill: the more patiently you work through the basics, the faster you progress later on.
A truly mature person knows how to switch freely between fast and slow, instead of being led by the nose by the pace of the times.
It is worth asking yourself: what in your life deserves to be deliberately slowed down?
On the subway, nearly everyone's head is bowed over a phone, fingertips flying across the screen.
We take in more information every day than in any previous era, yet the knowledge that actually settles in us is pitifully scarce.
The greatest harm of fragmented reading lies in how it robs us of the capacity for deep thought.
A long essay gets squeezed into a three-line summary, a classic gets boiled down to a five-minute video — and just like that, depth of thought is sacrificed.
Neuroscience research shows that a brain long accustomed to skipping and skimming gradually loses the patience to concentrate for extended stretches.

This is not to say fragmented reading is worthless — for picking up information it is admittedly convenient — but letting it replace systematic reading is like living on snacks instead of proper meals.
Reading a thick book is like holding a long conversation: you must slow your pace, weighing and debating with the author again and again.
The more violently the tide of information surges, the more we need a quiet island where we can simply read.
Why can some people keep going without any supervision, while others can barely move a step once rewards and punishments are removed?
Psychologists boil the answer down to "intrinsic drive" — that is, motivation that comes from within and needs no external reward.
A classic experiment makes the point well: researchers had two groups of children draw — one group got a prize for each finished drawing, the other got nothing at all.
Weeks later, the children who had been rewarded no longer wanted to draw once the prizes stopped, while the other group still drew with as much delight as ever.
External rewards admittedly work fast, but they quietly corrode a person's interest in the activity itself.
Genuine intrinsic drive usually comes from three elements: room for autonomy, the satisfaction of steadily improving, and a sense that the work is meaningful.
Whether it is learning a language or keeping up a sport, whatever is forced through by sheer willpower mostly ends half-finished, while whatever is driven by interest tends to flow on steadily.
Rather than forcing yourself to work hard, think it through honestly: why, for you, is this thing actually worth doing?
Language is not merely a tool for communication; it is, even more, the vehicle of thought: the language we think in often determines how we see the world.
Linguists once proposed a famous hypothesis: the structure of each language imperceptibly shapes the way its speakers perceive things.
For example, Chinese calls last month "the month above" and next month "the month below," as if time were a river flowing downward, whereas English habitually places the future in front of the body.

Another example: Chinese kinship terms are extremely fine-grained — a maternal uncle, a father's younger brother, and a father's elder brother each have their own word — so speakers must constantly attend to exactly how relatives are related.
This by no means implies that language is a prison locking thought firmly inside; it is more like a pair of glasses that makes certain details exceptionally clear.
People who master a second language often find that when they switch between the two, their personality and tone undergo a subtle change as well.
Learning a foreign language is not so much acquiring new words as acquiring another gaze with which to look at the world.
Next time you speak Chinese, take a moment to notice what this language is quietly steering you to pay attention to, and what it is letting you overlook.
The phrase 'great talent matures late' comes from the Tao Te Ching, meaning that true greatness often needs long years to ripen.
In an era that worships early success, these four characters feel like a dose of good medicine.
The painter Qi Baishi did not settle in Beijing until he was fifty-seven, was still reinventing his style at nearly sixty, and ultimately became the master of his generation.
He was by no means exceptionally gifted; rather, he honed his brushwork day after day for decades, slowly simmering loneliness into mastery.
Look at today, by contrast: many young people, if they have not 'succeeded' by thirty, conclude that they will never amount to anything.
In truth, life is not a sprint but a long marathon, and a slow starter does not necessarily lose at the finish line.
Society's clock is ticking, admittedly, but each of us lives in a time zone of our own.
Some flowers bloom in spring, while others wait until late autumn before bursting into full flower.
Rather than agonize over your age, quietly keep cultivating your craft; a great vessel is finished late precisely because it is so great.
In the era of information explosion, everyone is a receiver of information and, at the same time, a disseminator.
Social media has brought unprecedented convenience, but it has also given rumors wings.
A sensational piece of fake news often spreads faster and wider than a rigorous refutation.
Algorithms push content based on users' preferences, causing people to sink unknowingly into an 'information cocoon.'
Over time, people can only take in voices that match their own position, and social consensus becomes ever harder to form.
Media literacy has therefore become an essential basic ability for modern citizens.
It requires us to verify sources and distinguish truth from falsehood, rather than repost blindly.
Facing the flood of information on our screens, have you ever wondered: are we using the media, or is the media shaping us?
The ancients said: "Dripping water pierces stone."
A single drop of water is insignificant, yet day after day it can pierce through solid stone.
Learning a language is the same: it relies not on momentary enthusiasm, but on long-term accumulation.
Improving a little every day seems tiny, yet it will eventually converge into astonishing change.
So please believe in the power of persistence.
Foreigners newly arrived in China are often puzzled: the work is clearly excellent, yet Chinese people keep saying, "I did it poorly — please give me your guidance."
When praised, replying "oh, not at all" is almost instinctive; people also call their own writing "my clumsy work" and their own views "my shallow opinion."
This modesty is by no means hypocrisy; it is a way of conducting oneself in the world, deeply rooted in the Confucian tradition.
Confucius said, "When three people walk together, one of them is surely my teacher," reminding us that no matter how outstanding we are, we can still learn from others.
In the traditional mindset, flaunting your brilliance not only looks uncultivated but also easily attracts jealousy — as the saying goes, "the gun shoots the bird that sticks its head out."
Playing yourself down a little while talking the other person up thus became a form of etiquette for maintaining interpersonal harmony.
Times are changing, though: in today's job interviews, young people have learned to present their strengths with confidence.
If someone compliments your Chinese, you can either reply modestly, "Oh, I still have a long way to go," or simply smile and say "thank you" — two responses that stand for two different eras.
Think about it: in your culture, what is the best way to accept a compliment?
Two thousand years ago, an old man lived at the frontier; people called him Saiweng, and one day his horse ran off for no reason at all.
His neighbors came one after another to console him, but he said mildly: 'This is not necessarily a bad thing.'
Later, the horse returned leading a fine steed; his son broke his leg riding it; and when war broke out, the lame son was spared from conscription and kept his life.
The textbooks tell us the moral of the story is that 'fortune and misfortune depend on each other.'
But in my view, Saiweng's real gift was not foreseeing the future — it was his refusal to jump to conclusions.
Today we scroll past a piece of news and within seconds rush to like it or rage at it, slapping a label of 'good' or 'bad' on everything.
Yet life is a long film, and we keep trying to judge the entire plot from a single screenshot.
Saiweng's two little words — 'not necessarily' — are a rare kind of mental white space, leaving time itself room to explain.
The next time fate knocks at your door, take a page from the old man: don't applaud just yet — and don't sigh just yet either.
'The great way is utterly simple' comes from Daoist thought: the most fundamental truths of the universe can usually be stated in the plainest words.
Steve Jobs believed this deeply: he pared the phone down to a single button and hid all the complexity out of sight.
Simple, he said, is harder than complex, because you must polish your thinking until it is clear enough.
Our own lives, by contrast, are endless exercises in addition: wardrobes stuffed with clothes, calendars packed with meetings, phones loaded with apps.
We mistakenly assume that the more we own, the closer we are to happiness — and end up so crushed by material craving that we can hardly breathe.
In truth, cutting the complicated down to the simple is a higher-order skill: knowing what you do not want is harder than knowing what you want.
A mountain never explains its height, and the sea never shows off its depth.
True abundance often appears wearing the plainest face.
Only those who have learned to subtract can be said to have truly understood that the great way is simple.
During the Second World War, the Allies wanted to add armor to their bombers in order to reduce losses.
The statisticians examined the planes that made it back, found the wings riddled with bullet holes, and so proposed reinforcing the wings.
But a statistician named Wald argued the opposite: what needed reinforcing was the engine area, where the fewest bullet holes were found.
His reasoning was brilliant: a plane hit in the wings could still fly home, which meant those areas could take punishment; but a plane hit in the engine never got the chance to return at all.
This is the famous "survivorship bias": we see only the samples that survived, and overlook the silent failures.
This bias is everywhere in daily life: the media love stories of dropout billionaires, yet rarely mention the tens of thousands of founders who failed.
Some then conclude that education is useless — little knowing that this is precisely a case of being blinded by the survivors.
The next time you hear a success story, it is worth first asking: what happened to the people who did not succeed?
Only when the silent evidence is also brought into view can our judgments come close to the truth.
The rapid development of science and technology is changing human life at an unprecedented speed.
Artificial intelligence can diagnose diseases, and gene editing promises to cure stubborn hereditary illnesses — the prospects are inspiring.
However, technology is a double-edged sword, and the consequences of abusing it are equally unthinkable.
Algorithms may intensify bias, facial recognition may infringe on privacy, and once data is leaked it can never be recalled, like spilt water.
What is even more worrying is that the pace of technological iteration far exceeds the pace at which law and morality are updated.
Some advocate that technology is neutral, believing the problem lies not in the tool but in the person who uses it.
But in my view, designers ought to build ethical considerations into technology from the very beginning.
The ultimate purpose of technology is to benefit humanity, not to turn people into slaves of their tools.
When technology can do everything, we need all the more to keep asking: what should not be done?
Legend has it that Yugong of the North Mountain was nearly ninety, yet two great mountains, Taihang and Wangwu, lay across his doorway, forcing a long detour every time he went out.
So he led his sons and grandsons in chiseling rock and digging earth, resolved to move the two mountains away.
Zhisou of Hequ ridiculed him for overreaching, but Yugong replied: 'My descendants will go on without end, whereas the mountains will grow no taller — why worry that they cannot be leveled?'
The Emperor of Heaven, moved by his sincerity, ordered mighty gods to carry the mountains away on their backs.
As a child, I was taught that the story is about perseverance; as an adult, I read another layer of meaning in it.
Modern people love to run a cost-benefit analysis before doing anything: how many years to learn a language? How long before workouts show results?
The moment the payoff comes too slowly, we give up halfway and turn to hunt for shortcuts.
What makes Yugong admirable is precisely that he never asked 'Will I live to see the result?' — only 'Is this worth beginning?'
Perhaps the mountain can never be fully moved — but a person no longer held hostage by immediate gains and losses has already climbed the highest mountain of all, the one within.

已经 pairs with sentence-final 了 to say something has already happened or is already the case.
别…了
别 + verb + 了 tells someone to stop doing something: 别想了 (stop thinking).
别 + verb (+ 了) — don't…
别 gives a friendly command not to do something: 别忘了放盐。
上次 / 这次 / 下次
次 (cì) is the measure word for times or occasions: 上次 'last time', 这次 'this time', 下次 'next time', e.g. 下次再来.
首先……其次……另外……最后……
A set of connectors for listing points in order: 首先 (first), 其次 (second), 另外 (in addition), 最后 (finally). The text uses them to organize the interview tips.
A 还是 B — 'or' in questions
还是 offers a choice between two options and makes the sentence a question by itself — no 吗 needed: 你喝茶还是喝咖啡？
还是 vs 或者 — two kinds of 'or'
In QUESTIONS use 还是: 热的还是冰的? In statements use 或者: 我喝茶或者咖啡都可以.
送 + person + object
送 (sòng) takes two objects: first the person, then the thing given, e.g. 送他一本书 'give him a book (as a gift)'.
每 … 都 …
每 (měi) 'every' is almost always paired with 都 (dōu) before the verb, e.g. 我每天都喝茶.
每…都…
每 (every) is usually followed by 都 before the verb: 每个星期日都去。
sentence + 吧 (suggestion)
吧 at the end of a sentence turns it into a soft suggestion or invitation, e.g. 走吧 (let's go), 买吧 (go ahead and buy it).
吧 (suggestion)
吧 at the end of a sentence makes a soft suggestion: 穿那件红的吧。
从…开始
从 + time + 开始 means 'starting from…', e.g. 从今天开始 (starting today).
一 + measure word + 都/也 + 没/不 (not even one)
This pattern emphasizes 'not a single one': 一个人都没有 (not a single person), 一句话也不说 (not say a single word).
谁……都 + 不/没
A question word + 都 + negative means 'no one / nothing at all'.
不仅…还有… (not only… but also…)
Extends a statement beyond the first item: 交流的不仅是商品，还有思想、音乐和技术。
从 A 到 B — from A to B

从…开始

让 (to ask / let someone do something)

让 + person + verb

从…开始
从 + time + 开始 means 'starting from…', e.g. 从今天开始 (starting today).

把 + object + verb + 进/好
In a 把 sentence, a direction or result word like 进 or 好 follows the verb to show where the object ends up, e.g. 把书放进包里.
因为…，所以…
This pair links a reason and its result. Unlike English, Chinese often uses both words together, e.g. 因为下雨，所以我不去.
之所以…，是因为…
Puts the result first and the cause second: 'the reason why A is that B'. It highlights the explanation, e.g. 我之所以学汉语，是因为想去中国工作.
verb + 不懂
不懂 after 听 or 看 means you cannot understand what you hear or read: 听不懂 'can't understand (by listening)', 看不懂 'can't understand (by reading)'.
一点儿 — a little
一点儿 goes after the verb to soften the amount: 我会说一点儿汉语 = I can speak a little Chinese.
adj + 一点儿 (a little more...)
一点儿 after an adjective softens it: a bit cheaper, a bit bigger.
A 离 B + 远/近
离 measures the distance between two places: 我家离公司很近.
A 离 B + 近/远
离 marks the distance between two places; put 近 (close) or 远 (far) after it, e.g. 我家离商店很远.
上次 / 这次 / 下次

有人…,有人… — some people…, others…
Repeat 有人 to describe a lively scene: 有人跑步,有人唱歌 (some are jogging, some are singing).
像…一样 (just like)
像 + noun + 一样 compares something to another: 像家人一样 (just like family), 像春天一样暖和 (as warm as spring).
好像……一样

如果 introduces a condition and 就 introduces the result: 'if ..., then ...'. For example, 如果明天下雨，我们就不去.
A + 被 (+ B) + verb
The 被 passive puts the thing acted on first; the doer comes after 被 and can be vague, like 人 'someone'. For example, 书被人借走了 means 'the book was borrowed by someone'.
被称为… (to be called / known as…)
A passive pattern for naming: 其中最大的船被称为“宝船”。
……才 + verb
才 marks that something happens later than hoped or expected — 'not until'. It goes right before the verb, e.g. 他十点才起床.
直到……才……
"Not until A did B happen." 直到 marks the turning point, 才 stresses that B happened late: 直到生病住院，我才明白健康有多重要。
先…然后… links two actions in order: 'first ..., then ...'. Each part is a full verb phrase, e.g. 先吃饭，然后看书.
'First ..., then ...' — states the order of two planned actions.
先…然后… links two actions in order: 'first ..., then ...'. Each part is a full verb phrase, e.g. 先吃饭，然后看书.
先…然后… (first…, then…)
先 + action 1, 然后 + action 2 puts actions in order: 先刷牙洗脸，然后吃早饭。
把 + object + verb + result
The 把 structure moves the object before the verb to show what is done to it, with a result after the verb.
把 (bǎ) construction
Subject + 把 + object + verb + result moves the object before the verb to show what is done to it: 经理把我介绍给大家。
又 A 又 B links two qualities or results that are true at the same time: 'both A and B', e.g. 这个菜又便宜又好吃.
又 A 又 B — both A and B
Links two adjectives that are true at the same time: 新家又干净又安静。
对……满意
'Satisfied with ...' — 对 introduces the target of the feeling.
不用 + V
'no need to...' — a soft way to say something is unnecessary. Note the tone change: bú yòng.
用……来 + verb
'to (verb) by means of...' — names the standard or tool used for an action.
不但 A,还 B — not only… but also…
The second half must add MORE on top of the first: 不但会用筷子,还会做中国菜.
不但……而且……
"Not only A, but also B" — the second part adds something stronger: 我不但认识了一个新地方，而且认识了一个新的自己。
越来越 + adjective
越来越 means 'more and more', describing a quality that keeps growing over time, e.g. 他汉语说得越来越好.
越来越 (more and more)
越来越 + adjective describes something increasing over time: 越来越好 (better and better), 越来越冷 (colder and colder).
Adjective + 极了 — extremely…
极了 after an adjective makes it very strong: 漂亮极了。
'After ..., (then) ...' — 以后 follows a time or action; 就 signals that the next thing happens right away.
一边…一边… (doing two things at once)
一边 + verb, 一边 + verb describes two actions happening at the same time: 一边刷牙，一边找衣服。
一边……一边……
一边 A 一边 B describes two actions happening at the same time, each 一边 followed by a verb, e.g. 他一边吃饭一边看电视.
adj + 地 + verb
地 turns an adjective into an adverb describing how an action is done.
地 (adverbial marker)
Adjective + 地 + verb turns the adjective into an adverb describing how the action is done: 认真地回答 (answer seriously), 慢慢地走 (walk slowly).
看起来……其实……
'it seems... but actually...' — contrasts appearance with reality.
根据……来决定
'Decide according to...' — base a decision on a certain standard or situation.
还是 vs 或者 — two kinds of 'or'
In QUESTIONS use 还是: 热的还是冰的? In statements use 或者: 我喝茶或者咖啡都可以.
一直 + verb
一直 means 'straight' with direction verbs, or 'continuously' with other verbs.
A 比 B + adjective + 多了
比 compares two things; adding 多了 after the adjective means 'much (more) ...', a big difference, e.g. 今天比昨天冷多了.
adj + 多了
Placed after an adjective, 多了 means 'much more (adj)' — used for a big change compared with before.
一边…一边… (doing two things at once)
一边 + verb, 一边 + verb describes two actions happening at the same time: 一边刷牙，一边找衣服。
一边……一边……
一边 A 一边 B describes two actions happening at the same time, each 一边 followed by a verb, e.g. 他一边吃饭一边看电视.
为什么不……呢? — why not…?
A friendly way to make a suggestion: 你为什么不坐地铁呢?
就要…了 (about to)
就要 + verb + 了 means something is going to happen soon, often with a time word: 下个月我就要上班了。
就要……了
就要…了 says something is about to happen soon, often with a time word before it, e.g. 火车就要开了 'the train is about to leave'.
难道……吗？
A rhetorical question that expresses disbelief: 难道你觉得我射箭不好吗？
若…，难免…
A formal conditional with an unavoidable outcome: 'if A, then B can hardly be avoided'. 若 is the written equivalent of 如果; 难免 marks the result as natural and predictable. 例：若准备不足，难免出错。
什么时候……，什么时候……
Repeating a question word like 什么时候 turns it into 'whenever / whatever': the second clause happens at the same (unspecified) time as the first, e.g. 你什么时候来，我什么时候等你.
不是……就是……
'if not A, then B' — the situation is always one of two (often undesirable) options.
一 + V + 就是 + duration (once you start, it lasts…)
Emphasizes how long an action continues once begun: 一走就是几个月。
把 + object + 还给 + sb
In a 把-sentence the object moves before the verb to show what is being handled; 还给 'return to' then names the receiver, e.g. 请把书还给老师.
不只是……更是……
'not only... but even more...' — the second part is the deeper or more important point.
不仅…，更…
A climactic pair: 'not only A, but even more, B'. 更 upgrades the second clause above the first, stronger than plain 而且. 例：他不仅是老师，更是朋友。
甚至
甚至 means "even" and introduces the most extreme or surprising case in a list or statement, as in 我很累，甚至有点儿后悔养了它.
看起来……其实……
'it seems... but actually...' — contrasts appearance with reality.
靠 (to rely on)
靠 + noun expresses the means by which something is achieved: 他靠一双手把两个孩子养大了。
不仅……而且……
"Not only A, but also B" — the 而且 part adds something further and often more important, as in 不仅是美丽的风景，而且是那种让人慢下来的生活.
不仅…还… (not only… but also…)

哪怕 concedes an extreme or imperfect situation, and 也 states that the conclusion still holds: 哪怕只能做到一半，也比刷到半夜好。
先…然后… links two actions in order: 'first ..., then ...'. Each part is a full verb phrase, e.g. 先吃饭，然后看书.
先…然后… (first…, then…)
先 + action 1, 然后 + action 2 puts actions in order: 先刷牙洗脸，然后吃早饭。
A…，B 则…
Formal 则 marks a contrast between two subjects: 'A does X, while B does Y'. 例：有的孩子马上吃了糖，有的则熬过了等待。
Literary 则 contrasts two parallel clauses ('whereas; then'): 火太猛会烧干，火太小则煮不熟。
与其…，不如…
Compares two options and prefers the second: 'rather than A, it is better to B', e.g. 与其在家等着，不如出去找他.
与其…不如… (rather than A, better B)
Compares two options and prefers the second: 与其在大城市给别人打工，不如回家做自己的事业。
与其…，不如…
Compares two options and prefers the second: 'rather than A, it is better to B', e.g. 与其在家等着，不如出去找他.
与其…不如… (rather than A, better B)
Compares two options and prefers the second: 与其在大城市给别人打工，不如回家做自己的事业。
在我看来
'In my view / as I see it' — introduces the speaker's personal opinion, usually at the start of a concluding sentence.
只是…而已
Means 'merely..., that's all' — plays something down as nothing more than what is stated, e.g. 我只是开个玩笑而已.
所谓的……
'The so-called...' — introduces a term that you are about to define or explain.
所谓…，是指…
A formal defining pattern: 'what is called A refers to B'. Essays use it to introduce and then define a technical term. 例：所谓内卷，是指无意义的过度竞争。
'If you want to..., then...' — states a goal and the way to achieve it.
之所以…，是因为…
Puts the result first and the cause second: 'the reason why A is that B'. It highlights the explanation, e.g. 我之所以学汉语，是因为想去中国工作.
之所以…是因为… (the reason why… is that…)
States a result first, then its cause: 之所以要保护这些文化，是因为它们一旦消失，就永远无法恢复。
之所以…，是因为…
Puts the result first and the cause second: 'the reason why A is that B'. It highlights the explanation, e.g. 我之所以学汉语，是因为想去中国工作.
之所以…是因为… (the reason why… is that…)
States a result first, then its cause: 之所以要保护这些文化，是因为它们一旦消失，就永远无法恢复。
一方面…，另一方面…
Presents two sides or aspects of the same topic, like 'on the one hand... on the other hand...', e.g. 学汉语一方面有趣，另一方面也很难.
一方面……另一方面……
'On the one hand... on the other hand' — presents two contrasting sides of an issue, very common in argumentative writing.
一方面…，另一方面…
Presents two sides or aspects of the same topic, like 'on the one hand... on the other hand...', e.g. 学汉语一方面有趣，另一方面也很难.
一方面……另一方面……
'On the one hand... on the other hand' — presents two contrasting sides of an issue, very common in argumentative writing.
毕竟
Means 'after all' — reminds the listener of an essential fact that supports the speaker's point, e.g. 别怪他了，他毕竟还是个孩子.
不是……而是……
"Not A, but B" replaces a wrong idea with the right one: 你不是在记字，而是在认识一种古老的文化。
不仅……而且……
"Not only A, but also B" — the 而且 part adds something further and often more important, as in 不仅是美丽的风景，而且是那种让人慢下来的生活.
不得不
Means 'to have no choice but to do something' — the action is forced by circumstances, e.g. 天太晚了，我不得不回家.
先后 (in succession; one after another)
An adverb showing that events happened one after another over time: 先后有二十四位皇帝在这里生活。
凡是…都/大多… (every; whatever…)
凡是 sweeps in a whole category without exception: 凡是船队到过的地方，大多留下了友好的记忆。
……指的是……
'... refers to ...' — used to define a term or expression.
所谓…，是指…

关键在于……
'The key lies in...' — points out the decisive factor for solving a problem.

并不在于…，而在于…
A formal way to correct an assumption about where the essence of something lies: 'lies not in A, but in B'. 并不 makes the denial emphatic.
一旦…就… (once…, then…)
一旦 marks a condition that, once it happens, brings an irreversible result: 有些东西一旦失去，就再也找不回来了。
一旦…，就…
'Once/as soon as A happens, B follows' — presents B as the immediate, almost automatic consequence of A. 例：一旦养成习惯，就很难改掉。
既可以…，也可以…
Presents two equally valid options: 'you can either A or B'. 既…也… coordinates the alternatives without ranking them. 例：周末既可以在家休息，也可以出去走走。
Place + 里/前面 + 有 — there is / there are
To say something exists at a place, put the place word first, then 有: 学校里有很多同学。学校前面有一个商店。
一方面…，另一方面…
Presents two sides or aspects of the same topic, like 'on the one hand... on the other hand...', e.g. 学汉语一方面有趣，另一方面也很难.
对……满意
'Satisfied with ...' — 对 introduces the target of the feeling.
凡是…都… (every… without exception)
凡是 introduces a whole category, and 都 confirms the rule applies to all of it: 凡是好吃的面，后面都是几十年的耐心。
凡是…，就/都…
Means 'whatever / whoever / wherever...' — states a rule that applies to every case in a category, e.g. 凡是他说的话，大家都相信.
万一
'What if / in case' — introduces an unlikely but worrying possibility: 万一以后用得着呢？
把 + O + V + 了一遍
The 把 structure moves the object before the verb; 一遍 means doing the action once from start to finish.
愈发
'increasingly; all the more' — a written synonym of 越来越.
恐怕
Means 'I'm afraid that...' — expresses a worried estimate about something probably negative, e.g. 现在出发，恐怕已经来不及了.
难道……吗？
A rhetorical question that expresses disbelief: 难道你觉得我射箭不好吗？
到底 (after all; in the end)
In a question, 到底 presses for the real answer: 到底是我们在用它，还是它在用我们？
与其…，不如…
Compares two options and prefers the second: 'rather than A, it is better to B', e.g. 与其在家等着，不如出去找他.
与其…不如… (rather than A, better B)
Compares two options and prefers the second: 与其在大城市给别人打工，不如回家做自己的事业。
被称为… (to be called / known as…)
A passive pattern for naming: 其中最大的船被称为“宝船”。
与此同时 (at the same time)
A formal connector adding a simultaneous event: 与此同时，葡萄、西方的好马等也传入了中国。
与此同时
'At the same time; meanwhile' — a formal connector that adds a second, parallel statement to the previous sentence.
既不……又不……
'Neither... nor...' — links two negative situations that are true at the same time.
既…，又…
'Both A and B' — coordinates two merits (or faults) of the same thing, giving the sentence a balanced, essayistic rhythm. 例：这个办法既省钱，又省时间。
根据……来决定
'Decide according to...' — base a decision on a certain standard or situation.
据说… (it is said that…)
Reports information from hearsay or tradition, without naming the source: 据说长一百多米。
即使……也……
"Even if ... still ..." — the result holds even under an extreme condition: 即使你每天只学三五个字，一年以后你也会认识一千多个汉字。
In formal writing, 即 means 'that is; namely', introducing a definition or restatement of the term just mentioned, much like 'i.e.' in English.
关键在于……
'The key lies in...' — points out the decisive factor for solving a problem.
不等于…而是… (does not mean…, but rather…)
Rejects one interpretation and gives the correct one: 保护遗产不等于把它们放进博物馆，而是让它们继续活在人们的生活中。
…，所以…
所以 (suǒyǐ) introduces the result of what was said before: '..., so ...', e.g. 天气不好，所以我们不去了.
因为…，所以…
This pair links a reason and its result. Unlike English, Chinese often uses both words together, e.g. 因为下雨，所以我不去.
以…为例
A formal way to introduce an illustration: 'taking … as an example'. Common in expository writing before a concrete case. 例：以北京为例，房价十年翻了一倍。
并非…，而是…
An emphatic corrective pair: 'is by no means A; rather, it is B'. The literary 非 plus reinforcing 并 makes the correction feel authoritative. 例：沉默并非软弱，而是一种修养。
并非……,而是……
'is by no means..., but rather...' — a formal written pattern that rejects one description and asserts another.
并非…，而是…
An emphatic corrective pair: 'is by no means A; rather, it is B'. The literary 非 plus reinforcing 并 makes the correction feel authoritative. 例：沉默并非软弱，而是一种修养。
并非……,而是……
'is by no means..., but rather...' — a formal written pattern that rejects one description and asserts another.
如果 introduces a condition and 就 introduces the result: 'if ..., then ...'. For example, 如果明天下雨，我们就不去.
与其…，不如…
Compares two options and prefers the second: 'rather than A, it is better to B', e.g. 与其在家等着，不如出去找他.
若…，难免…
A formal conditional with an unavoidable outcome: 'if A, then B can hardly be avoided'. 若 is the written equivalent of 如果; 难免 marks the result as natural and predictable. 例：若准备不足，难免出错。
若…，难免…
A formal conditional with an unavoidable outcome: 'if A, then B can hardly be avoided'. 若 is the written equivalent of 如果; 难免 marks the result as natural and predictable. 例：若准备不足，难免出错。
首先……其次……另外……最后……
A set of connectors for listing points in order: 首先 (first), 其次 (second), 另外 (in addition), 最后 (finally). The text uses them to organize the interview tips.
看起来……其实……
'it seems... but actually...' — contrasts appearance with reality.
即使……也……
"Even if ... still ..." — the result holds even under an extreme condition: 即使你每天只学三五个字，一年以后你也会认识一千多个汉字。
使 + sb + verb
'to cause/make somebody do something' — a formal causative common in written argumentation.
并不在于…，而在于…

不在……,而在……
'lies not in..., but in...' — rejects a false cause and points to the true one.

一旦……便……
'once... then...' — a formal conditional often signaling serious or irreversible consequences.
一旦……，便/就……
'Once/the moment A happens, then B' — 便 is the literary equivalent of 就. 一旦回报来得太慢，我们便半途而废。
反之
'Conversely; on the other hand' — a formal connector that introduces the opposite case of what was just said. 例：每天读书使人进步；反之，每天熬夜刷手机透支健康。
竟然
"Surprisingly; unexpectedly" marks something contrary to what you would expect: 很多城市里长大的孩子竟然听不懂爷爷奶奶的方言了。
毕竟
Means 'after all' — reminds the listener of an essential fact that supports the speaker's point, e.g. 别怪他了，他毕竟还是个孩子.

In QUESTIONS use 还是: 热的还是冰的? In statements use 或者: 我喝茶或者咖啡都可以.

Classical 者 turns a verb or phrase into 'one who…', common in proverbs and formal writing. 例：知足者富 = 知足的人富有。

'Not necessarily' — a softer, more literary way to negate certainty than 不一定. 例：物质条件好了，幸福感却未必增长。
……指的是……
'... refers to ...' — used to define a term or expression.
V + 得 + 状态 (degree complement)
得 links a verb to a description of how the action happens.

'to be regarded as' — a formal passive structure very common in written Chinese.
依我看
'in my view' — introduces the writer's own opinion, often before a counter-argument.
未免
'rather; a bit too' — politely signals that a statement or judgment goes too far.
…，所以…
所以 (suǒyǐ) introduces the result of what was said before: '..., so ...', e.g. 天气不好，所以我们不去了.
因为…，所以…
This pair links a reason and its result. Unlike English, Chinese often uses both words together, e.g. 因为下雨，所以我不去.
莫过于
A formal superlative: 'nothing surpasses…; the greatest/most typical is none other than…'. Often follows 最…的 to crown one example above all others. 例：人生最大的幸福莫过于家人平安。
莫过于
A formal superlative: 'nothing surpasses…; the greatest/most typical is none other than…'. Often follows 最…的 to crown one example above all others. 例：人生最大的幸福莫过于家人平安。
固然…，但…
A concessive pivot: 'admittedly A, but B'. It grants the first point genuine validity before asserting that the second matters more. 例：钱固然重要，但健康更重要。

不妨 + verb
A soft, polite suggestion meaning 'might as well; there is no harm in doing something'. Common in essays when inviting the reader to try an idea.

'might as well; there is no harm in' — politely suggests trying something. 不妨学学那位老人。

Literary 'turning to look at… (by contrast)' — introduces a contrasting subject right after an example. 反观我们的生活，却在不断做加法。
愈发
'increasingly; all the more' — a written synonym of 越来越.
在我看来
'In my view / as I see it' — introduces the speaker's personal opinion, usually at the start of a concluding sentence.

'to appear in the guise of…' — literary 以 here means '用/带着'. 真正的丰盛，往往以极简的面目出现。
令人 + verb/adj
'makes one (feel)...' — a written pattern forming expressions like 令人振奋 (inspiring) and 令人担忧 (worrying).

A literary connector meaning 'little do they know; what they fail to realize is'. It introduces a truth that contradicts the belief just stated.
恰恰在于
'lies precisely in' — pinpoints the essence of something, often after words like 可贵、问题、区别. 愚公的可贵，恰恰在于他不问结果。
意味着
'To mean / to imply' — states the consequence of a fact: 这意味着能源、交通、工业都要发生巨大的变化。
